title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Living Virtually: Privacy in the Metaverse

texts.A: The transition from a 2D internet to an immersive 'Metaverse' is a privacy nightmare of unprecedented proportions. In a virtual world, companies aren't just tracking your clicks; they are tracking your eye movements, your gait, and even your physiological responses to stimuli. [37]While some argue that this data is necessary for a 'seamless' experience, I believe it represents the final frontier of surveillance capitalism.[/37] [39]If we don't establish 'human-centric' digital rights now, we will find ourselves living in a world where our very thoughts are being harvested for advertising.[/39]
texts.B: The Metaverse offers an incredible opportunity to redefine human connection, transcending physical and geographical boundaries. [38]I disagree with those who believe that the privacy risks are fundamentally different from those of current social media; we already give away vast amounts of data, and the Metaverse is simply an evolution of that trend.[/38] [40]By using blockchain and decentralized identity protocols, we can actually give users more control over their digital personas than they have in the real world.[/40] We should focus on the creative potential of these spaces rather than being paralyzed by fear.
texts.C: The real danger of the Metaverse is the potential for 'behavioral engineering.' If a company can control the environment you inhabit, they can subtly nudge your decisions in ways that are invisible to you. [37]I share the view that the level of biometric data collection required for VR is inherently invasive and cannot be 'anonymized' in any meaningful way.[/37] [38]Unlike those who see this as a 'simple evolution,' I believe the immersive nature of the Metaverse makes the psychological impact of data-mining far more profound.[/38] We are not just users of the Metaverse; we are the product.
texts.D: As with any new technology, the initial reaction to the Metaverse is one of alarmism. [40]I share the view that decentralized technologies will eventually solve the trust issues that plague our current centralized platforms.[/40] However, we must be realistic; most users are willing to trade a degree of privacy for a superior, more entertaining experience. [39]I am less convinced than some of my colleagues that 'digital rights' legislation can keep pace with the speed of innovation.[/39] The market, not the government, will ultimately decide which platforms succeed based on how well they protect their users' interests.

questions.question: Which expert has a similar view to A on the invasive nature of the data collected in the Metaverse?
questions.explanation: A describes the tracking of eye movements and physiology as a 'nightmare.' C shares this, stating that biometric data collection is 'inherently invasive' and cannot be anonymized.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a different opinion to B regarding whether the Metaverse represents a significantly new type of privacy threat?
questions.explanation: B believes the privacy risks are NOT fundamentally different from current social media. C disagrees, stating that 'unlike those who see this as a simple evolution,' the immersive nature makes the impact 'far more profound.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the likelihood of using legislation to protect users' digital rights?
questions.explanation: A insists we MUST establish digital rights now to prevent disaster. D is 'less convinced' that such legislation 'can keep pace with the speed of innovation,' preferring to trust the market.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's optimism about the role of decentralized technology in protecting privacy?
questions.explanation: B suggests that 'blockchain and decentralized identity' will give users more control. D also 'shares the view that decentralized technologies will eventually solve the trust issues.'
